1.synthesis: After the uproar ceases, Paul sends for the disciples, encourages them, and departs for Macedonia. He travels through those regions with many words of encouragement and comes to Greece, where he stays three months (Acts 20:1–3). When a Jewish plot against him on the point of sailing to Syria is discovered, he decides to return through Macedonia. He is accompanied by Sopater of Berea, Aristarchus and Secundus of Thessalonica, Gaius of Derbe, Timothy, Tychicus and Trophimus of Asia (Acts 20:3–5). They sail from Philippi after the days of Unleavened Bread and meet in Troas within five days (Acts 20:6).
1.historical_context: The seven companions named in Acts 20:4 represent the Gentile churches' delegation carrying their collection for Jerusalem—they are ambassadors of their communities, accompanying the gift to ensure its proper delivery. Paul's letters (Romans 15:25–28; 2 Corinthians 8:18–24) reveal the theological and practical significance of this collection: it is a tangible sign of Gentile-Jewish unity and a fulfillment of the Jerusalem Council's implicit expectation. The Jewish plot at Corinth (v.3) forces a route change—Paul's flexibility in altering plans to avoid danger continues. The 'we' narration resumes at Philippi (v.5), placing Luke with the party again.
1.literary_structure: This transitional section bridges the Ephesian crisis and the farewell journey to Jerusalem. The seven companions are named individually—an unusual level of detail that signals their importance as representatives of the Gentile mission. The Passover timing (days of Unleavened Bread, v.6) gives the journey toward Jerusalem a paschal frame: Paul is moving toward his own Passover crisis. The five-day sail from Philippi to Troas (v.6) contrasts with the two-day outward journey (16:11), reflecting normal wind conditions rather than the providential speed of the first arrival.
1.theological_application: The seven named companions carrying the collection represent the catholicity of Paul's Gentile mission—Bereans, Thessalonians, Derbean, Asians—a microcosm of the churches the mission has planted. Their accompaniment of the gift is an act of communal accountability: the churches are not sending money anonymously but sending representatives who can speak personally to the Jerusalem community about the Gentile churches' faith and generosity. The church's material generosity is always most powerful when accompanied by relational presence, not merely financial transfer.
1.christology: The collection for Jerusalem is, in Paul's theological interpretation, the fulfillment of Isaiah's vision of the nations bringing their wealth to Zion (Isaiah 60:5–6)—a christological action: the Gentile churches, incorporated into the covenant people through the risen Christ, now express that unity through material solidarity with the Jewish mother church. The journey toward Jerusalem carrying this gift moves in the same direction as Jesus' own fatal journey to Jerusalem—Paul's impending passion in the holy city is the shadow falling across this cheerful delegation of Gentile ambassadors.

7.synthesis: On the first day of the week at Troas, they gather to break bread; Paul, intending to depart the next day, talks until midnight. A young man named Eutychus, sitting in the window, is sunk in deep sleep and falls from the third story—picked up dead (Acts 20:7–9). Paul goes down, bends over him, takes him in his arms: 'Do not be troubled, for his life is in him' (Acts 20:10). After going up, breaking bread, and eating, he talks until dawn and departs. The young man is taken home alive; they are greatly comforted (Acts 20:11–12).
7.historical_context: The 'first day of the week' (v.7) is the earliest reference in Acts to Sunday as the day of Christian gathering—a practice attested elsewhere in 1 Corinthians 16:2 and Revelation 1:10. The 'breaking of bread' is the Lord's Supper embedded in a shared meal—here extended before a long journey. Paul's action over Eutychus deliberately echoes Elijah's restoration of the widow's son (1 Kings 17:21) and Elisha's restoration of the Shunammite's boy (2 Kings 4:34–35)—the same lying-upon gesture. The lamp-lit room (v.8) may explain the young man's drowsiness: lamps consumed oxygen.
7.literary_structure: The Eutychus episode is brief but carefully framed: it interrupts the farewell journey at Troas with a miracle that parallels Peter's raising of Dorcas (9:36–42). The miracle occurs within the Sunday liturgical gathering—word-proclamation extended until midnight—suggesting that the community's primary experience of resurrection power is within its worship. The communal meal after the restoration (v.11) and the 'greatly comforted' response (v.12) together form the resurrection's aftermath: eating and consolation, life restored and celebrated.
7.theological_application: Eutychus's restoration during the community's worship gathering—word, meal, and miraculous life-restoration in the same space—presents the Sunday assembly as the place where the resurrection's power is most tangibly experienced. The church gathers on the first day of the week because that is the day of resurrection; it gathers around word and table because these are the means of the risen Lord's presence; and within this gathering, life is occasionally restored in dramatic ways that signify the full resurrection to come. The community's 'greatly comforted' response is appropriate: they have witnessed in miniature the hope that animates all Christian worship.
7.christology: Paul's action over Eutychus—bending over him, embracing him, announcing life—extends the resurrection ministry of the risen Christ through his apostle. 'His life is in him' (v.10) is Paul's announcement before Eutychus shows signs of life, spoken in the confidence of the one who acts in Jesus' name. The Sunday Eucharist that frames this episode is itself the community's regular encounter with the risen Christ; Eutychus's restoration makes explicit what every Sunday gathering enacts implicitly: the presence of the risen Lord who gives life.

13.synthesis: Going ahead to the ship, the companions sail to Assos, where they take Paul aboard—he had arranged this, intending to go on foot (Acts 20:13). They sail on through Mitylene, past Chios, to Samos, and reach Miletus. Paul has decided to sail past Ephesus so as not to spend time in Asia, for he is hastening to be in Jerusalem by the day of Pentecost if possible (Acts 20:14–16).
13.historical_context: Paul's decision to walk the twenty miles from Troas to Assos while his companions sail may reflect a desire for solitude and prayer before the farewell speeches at Miletus—the last direct addresses he will give to his Asian churches. Miletus was the great port city of Ionia, some thirty miles south of Ephesus; by stopping there and sending for the Ephesian elders, Paul can farewell his closest church without the delays an Ephesian stop would create. The Pentecost deadline (v.16) drives the urgency—Paul wishes to be in Jerusalem for one of the three great pilgrimage festivals, consistent with his continued participation in the Jewish liturgical calendar. The deliberate overland walk from Troas to Assos—twenty miles while his companions sailed—may reflect pastoral preparation: the weight of the Miletus farewell and the Jerusalem journey ahead required the kind of solitary walking-prayer that ships and crowds cannot provide.
13.literary_structure: This brief travel notice establishes the logistics of the Miletus farewell while revealing Paul's deliberate pastoral strategy: bypassing Ephesus to avoid both delay and emotional entanglement, but not abandoning the Ephesian elders without a farewell. The itinerary—Assos, Mitylene, Chios, Samos, Miletus—is the 'we' narrator's eyewitness account, providing the geographical specificity of traveled memory. The Pentecost urgency gives the entire journey a liturgical urgency that recalls Jesus' own journey to Jerusalem for Passover.
13.theological_application: Paul's pastoral decision to bypass Ephesus rather than risk getting drawn into extended ministry there—choosing instead to summon the elders to Miletus—models the discipline of mission planning that holds to larger purposes even when immediate pastoral demands are compelling. Not every pastoral need can be addressed in every season; Paul's judgment that his Roman itinerary requires Jerusalem by Pentecost shapes which pastoral investments he makes in this window. The church's leaders face similar decisions about where to invest limited time and energy, requiring the same kind of priority-setting under Spirit-guidance.
13.christology: The Pentecost urgency is not merely calendrical but christological: Pentecost is the feast of the Spirit's outpouring, the festival that most directly recalls the risen Christ's gift to the church. Paul's desire to be in Jerusalem for Pentecost may reflect theological symbolism as well as practical logistics—the apostle to the Gentiles returning to the city of the Spirit's first outpouring, carrying the Gentile collection, at the festival of the Spirit. The risen Christ's purposes move through both calendrical and geographical convergences.

17.pericope_label: Paul's Farewell to the Ephesian Elders
17.synthesis: Paul sends from Miletus to Ephesus for the elders of the church. He rehearses his ministry among them: serving with humility, tears, and trials; not shrinking from declaring the whole counsel of God; testifying to both Jews and Greeks of repentance toward God and faith in the Lord Jesus (Acts 20:17–21). Now compelled by the Spirit, he is going to Jerusalem, not knowing what will happen there, except that the Spirit testifies that imprisonment and afflictions await him—but he does not count his life precious to himself (Acts 20:22–24). He declares that they will not see his face again; he is innocent of their blood, for he did not shrink from declaring the whole counsel of God (Acts 20:25–27). They must shepherd the flock over which the Holy Spirit has made them overseers; savage wolves will come (Acts 20:28–30). He commends them to God and to the word of his grace. His own example: he coveted no one's silver or gold; he worked with his own hands and gave to the weak (Acts 20:31–35).
17.historical_context: The Miletus address is Paul's only speech in Acts addressed to Christians rather than outsiders or opponents—it functions as his pastoral testament, the fullest statement of his ministry philosophy. 'The whole counsel of God' (v.27, boulē) echoes the divine plan-language throughout Luke-Acts. The warning about 'savage wolves' (v.29) and 'men speaking twisted things' from within the community (v.30) is the earliest Acts evidence of internal doctrinal threat to the Pauline churches. Paul's manual labor (v.34) and financial independence from the churches he served are themes confirmed throughout his letters (1 Corinthians 9:1–18; 1 Thessalonians 2:9).
17.literary_structure: The address is the structural counterpart to Peter's Pentecost sermon—Peter looks forward to what the Spirit will do; Paul looks backward at what the Spirit has done and forward at his own passion. The speech has three movements: Paul's past ministry reviewed (vv.18–21), his coming passion announced (vv.22–27), and the elders' future responsibilities charged (vv.28–35). The quotation of Jesus' saying 'It is more blessed to give than to receive' (Acts 20:35) is the only unrecorded Jesus saying quoted in Acts—a logion not found in the Gospels but preserved in Paul's oral tradition.
17.theological_application: 'I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable, and teaching you in public and from house to house' (Acts 20:20)—Paul's ministry model combines synagogue-style public teaching with the intimate instruction of household gatherings. The 'whole counsel of God' that he declares without shrinking includes the difficult themes—repentance, judgment, the cross—alongside the comforting ones. The church's pastoral leadership must resist the temptation to soften or omit the counsel that provokes resistance; the standard Paul sets is comprehensive and courageous proclamation regardless of reception. 'Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock' (Acts 20:28) puts self-examination first—the shepherd's own soul before the flock's.
17.christology: 'The church of God, which he obtained with his own blood' (Acts 20:28) is the most direct statement of the atonement's cost in Acts: the flock belongs to God and was purchased by divine blood—the incarnate Son's death. This is the theological ground of the elders' responsibility: they are overseers of what God bought at infinite cost. The logion about giving versus receiving (Acts 20:35) attributed to Jesus is the ethical application of a christological reality: the one who gave himself for us creates a community shaped by self-giving generosity rather than accumulation.

36.synthesis: When Paul finishes speaking, he kneels and prays with all of them. There is much weeping; they fall on Paul's neck and kiss him, sorrowing most of all for the word he spoke—that they will see his face no more. They accompany him to the ship (Acts 20:36–38).
36.historical_context: Kneeling in prayer (v.36) was not the standard Jewish posture (standing was normal) but appears in Acts at moments of particular solemnity or urgency (Acts 7:60; Acts 9:40; Acts 21:5). The embrace, the weeping, the escort to the ship—all reflect the depth of relational bonds formed over three years of shared ministry in Asia. The grief centers specifically on the word he has spoken—that they will not see his face again—indicating that the community has taken his prediction seriously and grieves not merely his departure but his permanent absence from their lives. The farewell at Miletus is one of the most emotionally detailed scenes in Acts.
36.literary_structure: The farewell scene is compact but emotionally weighty—three verses capturing the fullness of relational grief. It balances the commissioning scene of Acts 13:1–3 (sending out with prayer and laying on of hands) with an arrival at the journey's emotional cost: mission requires separation, and separation is genuinely painful. The 'much weeping' on both sides is not self-pity but the appropriate sorrow of those who love and must part. Luke's inclusion of this scene signals that the apostolic mission is not coldly strategic but relationally costly—deep bonds formed in shared mission are then broken by that same mission's demands.
36.theological_application: The Miletus farewell models the emotional reality of Christian community: genuine love, genuine grief at parting, and the willingness to release what one loves for the sake of the mission's larger purposes. Paul does not suppress the community's grief or his own; he weeps with them, prays with them, and then boards the ship. The church's discipleship must make space for the genuine cost of mission—not only the joy of conversion and growth but the sorrow of separation, the reality that mission's demands sometimes break deep bonds of love in order to extend that love farther. The escort to the ship is the community's final act of love: accompanying as far as they can go.
36.christology: The prayer that precedes the farewell—Paul kneeling with the elders—places the coming separation and suffering in the hands of 'God and to the word of his grace' (Acts 20:32) as Paul has already commended them. The farewell at Miletus is, at its deepest level, a commendation of both Paul and the elders to the risen Christ whose mission they share. The grief is real because the love is real; and the love is real because the risen Christ whose love they have experienced has made them into a community that reflects his self-giving. They weep because he has made them capable of loving that deeply.
